summary: Jeff Atwood explores the paradox that as computer hardware costs approach zero, electricity becomes the dominant expense—especially for companies like Google running massive datacenters. He examines Google's innovative approaches to power efficiency, including custom 90% efficient power supplies and strategically located datacenters in low-cost power regions. Atwood then applies this analysis to consumer PCs, finding that while efficient power supplies exist, the savings for individual users are marginal—about $30 per year in his calculations. He concludes that what's a strategic business imperative for Google becomes a matter of collective public good when multiplied across millions of consumer PCs worldwide.
key_points: Bill Gates predicted hardware costs would approach zero, and history proved him right—but this shifts the cost burden to electricity
Google builds custom 90% efficient power supplies and locates datacenters in regions with cheap power as strategic business decisions
Standard PC power supplies peak at 85% efficiency around 250 watts, but most desktop PCs idle at 120-150 watts where efficiency drops
For individual users, upgrading to an efficient power supply saves roughly $30/year—barely justifying the hardware cost over three years
What doesn't matter for individuals becomes significant public good when multiplied across millions of computers worldwide
money_quotes: If server power consumption grows 20 percent per year, the four-year cost of a server's electricity bill will be larger than the $3,000 initial price of a typical low-end server.
It's not hard to do. That's why to me it's personally offensive that standard power supplies aren't as efficient.
Your PC is statistically idle 99% of the time it is turned on.
The real challenge is to deliver 90%+ efficiency at typical idle power consumption levels – 120-150 watts.
I'd save a whopping thirty bucks per year. That's not even enough to cover the cost of the energy-efficient power supply!
All this tells us is that Google's problems aren't necessarily our personal problems.
Considering the millions upon millions of computers in the world, more efficient PC power supplies are also part of the greater public good.
key_insight: The economics of efficiency operate on completely different scales—what's a rounding error for individuals becomes a strategic imperative for corporations and an environmental necessity for society.
